results.possibleIdentity: Harley Jones was an African American video artist, educator, and technical consultant active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his pioneering work in video art and his role as an instructor at the Studio Museum in Harlem, where he conducted the museum's first video workshop in the early 1970s. Jones was closely associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit that facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers. He served as a technical consultant and video artist for E.A.T., notably participating in the development of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. His work often focused on the intersection of media technology and social community, providing access to video tools for local residents in Harlem. His legacy is preserved in the permanent collection and archives of the Studio Museum in Harlem, which has cited his estate in recent annual reports as a donor of art.

Harley Jones is a Welsh filmmaker and educator who was highly active in the experimental film scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He began his career as a film editor at the BBC and Scottish Television in the early 1960s. In 1966, he founded the Newport Film Unit at the Newport College of Art and Design in Wales, serving as its first course leader for 18 years. Under his leadership, the school became a major center for experimental and independent filmmaking in the United Kingdom. His own work includes the 1968 film 'Resurrection,' an impressionistic study of steam engines. Jones has been recognized for his contributions to the Welsh film industry and the preservation of its archives. In later years, he co-founded the community cinema 'Monico Movies' in Cardiff.

results.possibleIdentity: Tony House was a musician active in the late 1960s, specifically known as a drummer for the British beat band The Mojos. Following the original group's breakup in 1966, the band's singer Stuart Slater re-formed the line-up in 1967 with Eddie Harnett, Duncan Campbell, and Tony House. This version of the band signed with Liberty Records and released a single titled "Until My Baby Comes Home" in June 1968. During this period, the group toured West Germany and recorded sessions for the BBC. House remained with the band until 1969, when he was replaced by drummer Danny Barbour.

The description provided by the user (an artist associated with Experiments in Art and Technology active in the late 1960s and early 1970s) bears a strong resemblance to the multimedia artist Tony Martin (Anthony Martin, born 1937). Martin was a pioneer of light shows and interactive environments who collaborated extensively with E.A.T. and David Tudor. He worked on the design and programming of the E.A.T. Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World Exposition (Expo '70) in Osaka, Japan. One of his notable projects involves an 'incredible assemblage' of a house in Kenilworth, which is frequently referred to as 'Tony's house' in creative and scholarly reflections. This 'house' project serves as a collaborative site where diverse materials and incongruent pasts coalesce into a work of art.
